did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures (EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, and MobileNetV2) for automated melanoma detection using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dermoscopic image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and presents original experimental results, including the training and evaluation of three specific CNN architectures (EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, MobileNetV2) on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset, along with primary data collection from a survey of 100 clinicians analyzed via Spearman correlation.

title: Performance Analysis of Convolutional Neural Network Architectures for Automated Melanoma Detection
journal: International Journal of Emerging Research in Engineering and Technology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of three pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network architectures—EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, and MobileNetV2—for automated melanoma detection using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dermoscopic image dataset. The models were assessed on standardized preprocessing, data augmentation, and training conditions, with MobileNetV2 achieving the highest accuracy of 0.9829. In addition to technical metrics, the research incorporated a survey of 100 clinicians to analyze adoption intentions based on the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) and trust-ethics frameworks. Spearman correlation analysis revealed that acceptance of AI-based detection positively correlated with performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, trust, and ethical/privacy concerns. The results indicate that high technical performance must be combined with trust, ethical transparency, and clinician acceptance to facilitate the real-world implementation of AI-based diagnostic tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for automated detection in the study. The paper classifies malignant melanoma into four subtypes in the introduction (Acral Lentiginous, Nodular, Lentigo Malignant, Superficial Spreading) but the experimental task on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset is binary classification of melanoma vs. benign lesions.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The study notes the dataset is imbalanced with many true negatives (benign cases) and very few true positives (melanoma cases).
Mentioned in the introduction as a serious skin disease alongside melanoma and squamous cell carcinoma, but not studied in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction as a serious skin disease alongside melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, but not studied in the experiments.
Mentioned in the context of other studies (Orhan and Yavşan, 2023) keeping tabs on nevi, but not a primary label in this paper's SIIM-ISIC 2020 experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma (malignant) versus benign lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'automated melanoma detection' using the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset. The top layer was changed to 'binary melanoma classification'. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. benign). Metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC-ROC, which are standard for classification tasks. The dataset is described as imbalanced with true negatives (benign cases) and true positives (melanoma cases).

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset collected using standardized techniques from many institutions, containing high-quality images with metadata for demographics and lesion location. It is characterized by extreme class imbalance (1.76% melanoma) reflecting real-world screening conditions.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, and MobileNetV2 models after preprocessing (resizing, quality control, normalization) and data augmentation.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the trained models' performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the strength and direction of monotonic relationships between independent variables (performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, trust, ethical concerns) and the dependent variable (acceptance and willingness to use AI-based Melanoma Detection tools) based on survey data from 100 clinicians.
To determine the statistical significance of the Spearman correlation coefficients, with a threshold of p ≤ 0.01 reported for significant associations.
To summarize the performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR) across various experimental trials for the CNN models.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no monotonic association between the independent variables (performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, trust, ethical/privacy concerns) and the dependent variable (acceptance and willingness to use AI-based Melanoma Detection tools).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a positive monotonic association between the independent variables (performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, trust, ethical/privacy concerns) and the dependent variable (acceptance and willingness to use AI-based Melanoma Detection tools).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported statistically significant positive correlations (p ≤ 0.01) between clinician acceptance of AI-based melanoma detection and factors including performance expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence, facilitating conditions, trust, and ethical/privacy concerns. This indicates that these technological and trust-related factors are determinants of clinicians' intentions to adopt the technology.

SIIM-ISIC 2020.notes: The dataset is characterized by extreme class imbalance (1.76% melanoma). All models showed high accuracy and specificity but very low sensitivity and precision, indicating difficulty in identifying melanoma cases. ResNet50 and MobileNetV2 predicted zero true positives for melanoma in the reported mean metrics. EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest AUC-ROC (0.5523) among the three.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from ImageNet were used for all three models. For EfficientNetB0, the first layers were frozen initially. For ResNet50 and MobileNetV2, ImageNet-pretrained weights were employed.

EfficientNetB0.description: CNN optimized by scaling depth, breadth, and resolution via a compound scaling factor. Uses 5.3 million parameters.

ResNet50.description: Residual Network with 50 layers. Uses 25.6 million parameters.

MobileNetV2.description: CNN designed for mobile devices with low computing effort. Uses 3.5 million parameters.

transfer_learning_strategy.stage_1: Training classification head with convolutional layers frozen
transfer_learning_strategy.stage_2: Unfreezing deeper layers with reduced learning rates for fine-tuning

classification_or_output_head.modification: The final/topmost classification layer was substituted/changed to detect melanoma (binary output).
classification_or_output_head.initial_state: First layers frozen in EfficientNetB0 during initial phase.

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: Geometric scaling to standard size of 224 x 224 pixels to maintain equal input size while preserving lesion structure.

Quality Control: Automated evaluation of sharpness, evenness of illumination, and artifact detection (e.g., hair and ruler marks), followed by visual inspection. Images below predefined quality thresholds were eliminated.
Normalization: Min-max scaling of pixel intensity values from range [0, 255] to [0, 1] to ensure numerical stability and consistent gradient updates.

Data Augmentation: Application of geometric transformations (random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips, zooming, spatial shifting) and photometric transformations (controlled brightness variation) exclusively to the training set.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set only to prevent overfitting and data leakage. Techniques included random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips, zooming, and spatial shifting. Clinically valid ranges were limited.
Applied to the training set only. Included controlled brightness variation.

conclusions: MobileNetV2 achieved the highest accuracy (0.9829) among the tested architectures (EfficientNetB0: 0.9817, ResNet50: 0.9804) for binary melanoma classification on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.

All three CNN models exhibited high specificity and accuracy but very low sensitivity and precision, indicating significant difficulty in identifying melanoma cases due to the extreme class imbalance (1.76% melanoma) in the dataset.
ResNet50 and MobileNetV2 predicted zero true positives for melanoma in the reported mean metrics, while EfficientNetB0 showed minimal detection capability (Sensitivity: 0.0094).

Spearman correlation analysis of a survey of 100 clinicians revealed statistically significant positive correlations (p ≤ 0.01) between the acceptance of AI-based melanoma detection and performance expectancy (r = 0.540), effort expectancy (r = 0.591), social influence (r = 0.571), facilitating conditions (r = 0.636), trust (r = 0.537), and ethical/privacy concerns (r = 0.544).
Technological and trust-related factors are determinants of clinicians' intentions to adopt AI-based diagnostic tools.
High technical performance must be combined with trust, ethical transparency, and clinician acceptance to facilitate the real-world implementation of AI-based diagnostic tools.
The study is limited by its reliance on a single publicly available dataset and the lack of external clinical validation.
important_remarks: The study exhibits severe class imbalance (1.76% melanoma prevalence), leading to critically low sensitivity (0.0094 for EfficientNetB0, 0.0000 for ResNet50 and MobileNetV2) and precision, despite high accuracy (>0.98).
ResNet50 and MobileNetV2 predicted zero true positives for melanoma in the reported mean metrics, indicating a failure to detect the target class in the majority of trials.
The research relies on a single publicly available dataset (SIIM-ISIC 2020) and lacks external clinical validation, limiting generalizability.
Clinician acceptance data is derived from a self-reported survey of 100 clinicians, which may not reflect actual real-world clinical deployment behaviors.

The paper acknowledges that high technical performance must be combined with trust, ethical transparency, and clinician acceptance for real-world implementation, highlighting a gap between technical metrics and clinical utility.
